title: Zubayrid Islam: The Caliphate That Governed While Others Performed
author: Mustafa Al-Husayni
excerpt:
A Essay in Islamic Political History — The Last Subaltern

Preface: Why We Name Names
History is not a neutral record. It is a document produced by survivors, and in the case of early Islamic political history, the survivors were the Umayyads who destroyed Ibn al-Zubayr, the Abbasids who replaced the Umayyads, and the theological traditions — Sunni and Shia alike — that each had institutional reasons to marginalize the Zubayrid project. The history we have inherited is their history. The silences in it are their silences.
This essay refuses those silences. It names the figures who shaped the crisis Ibn al-Zubayr was responding to — including Ali ibn Abi Talib, including Husayn ibn Ali, including Muawiya and Yazid and Hajjaj ibn Yusuf — because the evasion of names is the evasion of argument, and this essay intends to argue.
The argument is this: Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr built the most morally serious political project of the early Islamic world, governed it for nearly a decade across a vast territory, and was destroyed not because he was wrong but because he was inconvenient — to the Umayyads who wanted dynasty, to the Alid tradition that wanted theological infallibility, and to a historical memory that found his complexity harder to manage than a martyr's clean death or a monarch's clean succession.
We intend to correct that.

I. The Crisis Ali Could Not Resolve
To understand Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr, you must first understand what he was responding to — and that requires an honest account of the political catastrophe that Ali ibn Abi Talib's caliphate both inherited and, in critical ways, deepened.
Uthman ibn Affan was murdered in 656 CE by rebels who had besieged his house for weeks while the Muslim community — including Ali — failed to intervene decisively. The murder was not a secret. The killers were known. Many of them subsequently joined Ali's army and became part of his administration in Kufa. This was the foundational political failure from which everything else followed.
When Aisha, Talha, and Zubayr demanded that Ali prosecute Uthman's killers before consolidating his caliphate, they were making a demand that was morally unimpeachable and politically straightforward: justice before legitimation. They were insisting that a caliphate which opens by absorbing the men who murdered the previous caliph has already made a statement about its relationship to accountability — and that statement is damning.
Ali's response was deferral. He argued that the situation was too unstable for prosecutions, that unity was the priority, that justice would come in time. These are arguments that every government which has ever protected its own criminally-connected supporters has made, in every era, in every civilization. They are not arguments. They are postponements. And the Muslim community had no guarantee, looking at the composition of Ali's inner circle, that the postponement would ever end.
We are not arguing that Ali was a corrupt man. We are arguing that he made a catastrophic political choice — that by failing to separate himself clearly and immediately from Uthman's killers, he made the demand for accountability appear to be the aggression, and his own position appear to be the legitimate order being disturbed. That inversion — where the demand for justice becomes the destabilizing force — is one of the oldest tricks of power. Ali may not have intended it. But it is what happened.
Aisha, Talha, and Zubayr refused the inversion. They marched to Basra. They raised the question publicly and formally, in the only language that seventh-century Arabian politics provided for raising questions: armed opposition.
The Battle of the Camel was the result.

II. The Battle of the Camel Was Right
We will state this plainly, because the evasion of plain statement on this point has distorted Islamic political thought for fourteen centuries: the cause at the Battle of the Camel was just.
Talha, Zubayr, and Aisha were not power-hungry opportunists. Talha was one of the ten promised Paradise. Zubayr was the Apostle's companion from childhood. Aisha was the Mother of the Believers, whose religious authority in the community was second to none. These were not fringe figures manufacturing a grievance. These were the foundational generation of Islam, and they looked at what had happened to Uthman and what was happening in its aftermath and said: this is not acceptable.
The demand they made — prosecute the killers of the Caliph before claiming the authority of Caliph — was not a demand for their own elevation. It was a demand for the principle that power answers to law. That the man at the top of the political order is not exempt from the moral order. That justice is not a luxury to be addressed after stability is secured, because stability secured through impunity is not stability — it is the institutionalization of crime.
They lost the battle. Thousands died. The loss is a tragedy. The loss does not retroactively make the cause wrong.
Let us be precise about the logical structure here, because it matters enormously. The argument that the Battle of the Camel was unjustified because it ended in bloodshed is the argument that outcome determines righteousness — that because the demand for accountability produced a violent confrontation, the demand itself was the error. This is precisely backwards. The violence was produced by Ali's refusal to meet the demand, not by the demand itself. When a legitimate demand is refused, and the refusal is enforced militarily, the blood produced by that enforcement belongs to the refusal, not the demand.
Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr was there. He fought in that battle. He watched his father die, watched Talha die, watched Aisha — the Mother of the Believers — led away from a battlefield that had been forced upon them. He drew from that experience not humiliation but clarity: that the demand for accountability is not negotiable, not deferrable, and not discredited by the violence that power deploys to suppress it.
That clarity became the moral architecture of everything he subsequently built.

III. Muawiya, Yazid, and the Monarchical Usurpation
The irony of Muawiya ibn Abi Sufyan is that he won using the same rhetoric that Aisha and the Zubayrids had deployed — the demand for justice for Uthman's murder — while pursuing entirely different ends.
Aisha, Talha, and Zubayr wanted accountability. They wanted the killers prosecuted and the principle of legal answerability established at the foundation of the new caliphate. Muawiya wanted Syria and then everything else. He used the language of justice for Uthman as a political instrument, not as a moral commitment. The proof is what he did once he had power: he made no meaningful effort to prosecute Uthman's killers. He simply became caliph and then, in the act that definitively ended the consultative tradition of early Islamic governance, appointed his son Yazid as his successor.
This was not a transition. It was a theft. The caliphate is not a monarchy. It had never been formalized as a monarchy. The mechanism of its transfer — whatever one thinks of the specific choices made in 632, in 634, in 644, in 656 — had always retained the form of communal recognition, of bay'ah extended by those with standing to extend it. Muawiya abolished that form without having the honesty to say he was abolishing it. He simply presented the community with his son as a fait accompli, and used the threat of military force to secure the pledges he needed.
Yazid ibn Muawiya was not a caliph. He was a crown prince who had been installed in a position the Islamic tradition had no legitimate mechanism for creating. His claim to obedience rested on nothing except his father's decision and the army that enforced it.
Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr refused that obedience. So did Husayn ibn Ali. So did significant portions of the Muslim community. The refusal was not rebellion. It was the correct response to an illegitimate demand.
What happened next defines the moral landscape of the entire period.

IV. Husayn at Karbala: The Sanctified Refusal
In 680 CE, Husayn ibn Ali — the grandson of the Prophet, the son of the fourth caliph, a man whose claim to moral authority in the Muslim community was among the strongest possible — marched toward Kufa with a small party of family and companions, responding to what he believed was a genuine popular uprising waiting to receive him.
The uprising was not waiting. The Kufans who had invited him capitulated before he arrived, pressured into submission by the Umayyad governor Ibn Ziyad. Husayn found himself at Karbala with seventy-two fighters facing an Umayyad army in the thousands. He was offered the chance to submit. He refused. He and nearly all the men with him were killed. The women and children of his household were taken captive to Yazid's court in Damascus.
The massacre at Karbala is one of the most consequential events in Islamic history — not primarily because of what it was, but because of what it became in the imagination of the Muslim world. It became the template for legitimate resistance: the pure sacrifice, the refusal to submit to tyranny even unto death, the willingness to be destroyed for a principle and to transmit that principle through destruction. Husayn's death sanctified his cause in a way that victory never could have. It became an argument in itself — an argument made not in words but in blood, that has proven more rhetorically powerful than anything written in the fourteen centuries since.
We honor Husayn's courage. His refusal to acknowledge Yazid's legitimacy was correct. His willingness to die rather than submit is not something this essay quarrels with.
But we must name what the sanctification of Karbala did to Islamic political consciousness — and what it prevented from being taken seriously.
The martyrdom model, once established at its fullest emotional and theological intensity at Karbala, created a hierarchy of resistance in which dying for a principle is more valorized than governing by one. The martyr's authority is perfect and permanent because it is sealed. He cannot disappoint. He cannot compromise. He cannot make the wrong decision about a tax collection or a military appointment or a tribal dispute. His record ends at the moment of maximum moral clarity, and that moment is preserved unchanged across centuries.
Governance doesn't work like that. Governance is impure by nature. It requires choices under conditions of incomplete information, trade-offs between competing goods, accommodations with realities that do not align with principles. A man who governs for nine years will inevitably have things in his record that can be criticized. A man who dies at the right moment will not.
Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr chose to govern for nine years.

V. The Zubayrid Caliphate: What Was Actually Built
Following Yazid's death in 683 CE and the subsequent crisis of Umayyad authority, Ibn al-Zubayr emerged as the acknowledged caliph across the greater part of the Muslim world. This is not a claim. This is the historical record, buried under Umayyad-derived historiography but recoverable from it.
The Hijaz — Mecca and Medina, the two most sacred cities in Islam — was under his direct control. He administered the pilgrimage. He maintained the Haramayn. Egypt recognized his authority. Iraq, including Kufa and Basra, pledged allegiance to him. Large portions of Khurasan came under his jurisdiction. Yemen submitted to his governors. For the period between 683 and the consolidation of Abd al-Malik's counter-campaigns in the late 680s, Ibn al-Zubayr was the caliph of the majority of the Muslim world.
He was not a rebel holding a corner of Arabia and calling it a caliphate. He was the functioning head of state of an actual, administered, functioning empire.
Let the implications of this be stated plainly. The narrative in which the Second Fitna is a period of Umayyad stability disrupted by Zubayrid rebellion is false. The accurate description is this: there were two caliphates. One was grounded in hereditary Umayyad claim, backed by Syrian military power, and funded by the revenues of the territories it controlled. The other was grounded in the moral credibility of its claimant, his lineage, his record of opposition when opposition cost something, and the communal recognition of regions that had chosen him without being coerced into it. That second caliphate — the one that required no army to secure its bay'ah, that was extended allegiance because people found its claimant worthy — was Ibn al-Zubayr's.
The Umayyad caliphate won because it had Hajjaj ibn Yusuf and the Syrian army and the willingness to bombard Mecca.
Ibn al-Zubayr's caliphate was more legitimate. It lost anyway.

VI. What Distinguished Him From Every Other Claimant
The early Islamic civil war period produced multiple competing frameworks for legitimate authority. It is worth specifying precisely where Ibn al-Zubayr stood in relation to each of them, because his position is distinct from all of them — and the distinction is the point.
The Alid claim — which would eventually crystallize into Shia political theology — rested on the argument that legitimate authority in the Muslim community belongs by divine designation to the descendants of the Prophet through Ali and Fatima. This claim evolved over time and took various forms, but its essential logic is theological: the Imam is designated by God, and this designation is transmitted through bloodline in a manner that carries spiritual authority inaccessible to ordinary political processes. The Alid claimant does not derive his legitimacy from communal recognition. He possesses it independently of whether the community recognizes it or not.
Ibn al-Zubayr rejected this framework entirely — not by arguing against it explicitly, but by operating in a completely different register. He claimed no divine designation. He asserted no inherited spiritual authority. His legitimacy was horizontal, not vertical: it came from the community, through bay'ah extended by people who found him credible, not from God through a bloodline that placed him beyond question.
The Umayyad claim rested on tribal prestige, administrative continuity, and ultimately the hereditary principle that Muawiya had installed. It was legitimacy as possession — the claim that ruling makes you the rightful ruler, that succession from a ruling family is itself a sufficient basis for authority.
Ibn al-Zubayr rejected this too. He was precisely not making a dynastic claim. He was not saying that the son of Zubayr has a hereditary right to the caliphate. He was saying that he had earned the trust of the community through his moral record, his lineage of principled opposition, and his willingness to refuse power that was not legitimately constituted — and that this trust, expressed through bay'ah, was the basis of his authority.
The Kharijite claim rested on moral absolutism — the argument that legitimate authority belongs only to those who meet an absolute standard of righteousness, and that any deviation from that standard, at any point in the record, disqualifies a claimant entirely.
Ibn al-Zubayr rejected this as well, for reasons that were both personal and principled. Personally, he could not accept a framework that condemned his father and the Mother of the Believers without remainder. Principally, he understood that a political order that can only be led by those who have never erred in conditions of genuine moral complexity is a political order that cannot be led at all. The Kharijite standard was not a criterion for leadership. It was a mechanism for permanent opposition to every conceivable leader.
What Ibn al-Zubayr was offering was something none of these frameworks provided: accountable authority. Leadership grounded in earned trust, answerable to the community that extended that trust, legitimate because of its conduct rather than its bloodline or its divine designation or its purity. Power that must keep earning its standing, or lose it.
This is the hardest kind of authority to maintain. It has no theological insurance. It has no hereditary inertia. It rests entirely on the ongoing judgment of the governed. Every decision is exposed. Every error is an argument against you.
Ibn al-Zubayr chose this anyway. Against an empire with unlimited resources and no equivalent scruple.

VII. The Kharijite Divergence and the Question of the Camel
The break between Ibn al-Zubayr and the Kharijites is among the most intellectually significant moments of the Second Fitna, because it reveals with unusual clarity what his project actually was — and was not.
The Kharijites shared his opposition to Umayyad tyranny. They despised Yazid. They had fought at Siffin and broken with Muawiya over the arbitration. They had grievances with every established power. In the immediate aftermath of Yazid's death, some of them initially aligned with Ibn al-Zubayr, finding in him the most credible alternative to Umayyad restoration.
But the Kharijites required of their leaders total moral clarity — meaning, in practice, the condemnation of every figure in early Islamic history who had participated in what they judged to be wrongdoing. Applied to the Battle of the Camel, this meant that Talha and Zubayr — men who had fought for accountability for Uthman's murder — were to be condemned as transgressors, because they had raised arms against Ali, who the Kharijites recognized as the legitimate caliph at the time.
Ibn al-Zubayr would not condemn them. He could not, and he would not.
He could not because Zubayr was his father. He would not because the condemnation would have been false — because the men who raised the demand for justice at the Battle of the Camel were not transgressors. They were right. They acted on principle, under conditions of genuine moral urgency, in pursuit of an accountability that was never delivered. To condemn them because the confrontation produced bloodshed is to hold the victims of Ali's failure accountable for the consequences of that failure. It is blame misplaced at the precise point where the misplacement serves power.
Ibn al-Zubayr's refusal was not sentiment masquerading as principle. It was principle expressed with the clarity that only someone with a personal stake in the question can fully achieve. He knew what his father had stood for. He knew what Aisha had stood for. He knew that the demand they had raised was just. And he refused to perform a condemnation of that demand in order to satisfy a political faction's appetite for moral absolutes.
The Kharijites departed. Good. Any coalition that requires you to lie about your father's righteousness in order to maintain it is a coalition not worth maintaining.

VIII. The Fall of Mecca and the Man Who Bombarded the Ka'aba
In 692 CE, Abd al-Malik ibn Marwan — the Umayyad caliph who had consolidated his power by defeating the various provincial revolts against his authority — dispatched Hajjaj ibn Yusuf to deal finally with Ibn al-Zubayr.
Hajjaj ibn Yusuf is celebrated in parts of the Islamic historical tradition as a great administrator and a strong ruler who brought order to a chaotic period. This celebration tells us something about what that tradition values. Hajjaj ibn Yusuf besieged Mecca. He erected catapults on the surrounding hills and fired projectiles at the Ka'aba — at the holiest structure in Islam, the direction of every Muslim's prayer, the house that Ibrahim is said to have built. He did this to kill one man.
The Ka'aba was damaged. Ibn al-Zubayr continued to resist. When the Umayyad forces finally breached the city, Ibn al-Zubayr — who was in his seventies and who had been offered terms of surrender which would have spared his life — chose to fight. He died fighting, within the Haram, within sight of the structure his enemies had bombarded.
Hajjaj had his head cut off and sent to Damascus. His mother, Asma bint Abi Bakr — the daughter of Abu Bakr, the first caliph, then in her extreme old age — reportedly looked at her son's body and said: the horseman has dismounted.
This is who the Umayyad state killed in its reassertion of control over the Muslim world. A man whose mother was the daughter of the first caliph. A man whose father had been among the Prophet's closest companions. A man who had administered Mecca and Medina for a decade and died defending them against bombardment.
The man who ordered the bombardment of Mecca is remembered as a capable administrator. The man who died defending it is remembered as a failed rebel. This inversion — this precise reversal of the moral reality — is not accidental. It is the product of a historiographical tradition that was written by the people who benefited from Hajjaj's assault and needed it to look like restoration rather than sacrilege.
It was sacrilege. We name it as such.

IX. What the Alid Tradition Did with His Memory
The Shia tradition — the tradition that most persistently and powerfully opposed Umayyad rule and that maintained the strongest counter-narrative to Umayyad historical legitimacy — might have been expected to preserve and honor the Zubayrid project. It did not.
The reason is structural. The Alid framework, as it developed after Karbala, required that legitimate Islamic authority flow through a specific bloodline — the descendants of the Prophet through Fatima and Ali. This framework could not accommodate Ibn al-Zubayr, whose claim rested on entirely different grounds: the earned trust of the community rather than the inherited designation of the Imam.
More specifically, the Alid tradition had to manage the awkward fact that the Battle of the Camel — the founding event of the Zubayrid moral genealogy — was fought against Ali. Whatever one thinks of Ali, the Alid tradition is committed to his legitimacy as the rightful caliph of his period. It cannot simultaneously honor the men who fought to hold him accountable for failing to prosecute Uthman's killers. The Zubayrid argument, taken seriously, would require acknowledging that Ali's handling of the post-Uthman crisis was a moral failure — and that Aisha and the Zubayrids were right to press him on it.
The Alid tradition has never made that acknowledgment. Instead, it has characterized the Battle of the Camel as a rebellion by power-seekers against the legitimate Imam — the precise framing that was politically convenient for Ali's supporters in 656 CE and remained institutionally entrenched in Alid historiography ever after.
Ibn al-Zubayr was therefore erased from two directions simultaneously: by the Umayyad historiography that portrayed him as a rebel against the rightful succession, and by the Alid historiography that portrayed his founding event as an unjust war against the rightful caliph. Both traditions had reasons to make him disappear. Together, they largely succeeded.

X. Why This Still Matters
The questions that the Zubayrid experiment raised are not antiquarian. They are the questions that every Islamic political tradition — and indeed every political tradition that grounds its legitimacy in moral terms — has to answer or evade.
Can authority be legitimate without being either divinely designated or dynastically inherited? The Zubayrid answer was yes — it can rest on earned communal trust. This answer has never been tried at scale since 692 CE in the Islamic world, not because it was proven wrong, but because every power that has governed that world since has found it inconvenient.
Can governance maintain moral seriousness without collapsing into either the purity of martyrdom or the corruption of dynasty? The Zubayrid answer was yes — with difficulty, imperfectly, under permanent pressure, but yes. This answer has been systematically suppressed by a tradition that valorizes the martyrdom of Husayn — which requires no governance — and tolerates the dynasticism of the caliphates — which requires no morality — while finding no conceptual place for the governance of conscience that Ibn al-Zubayr attempted.
Can a political tradition hold its own early history honestly — acknowledging that figures it honors made serious errors — without collapsing into either hagiography or total condemnation? The Zubayrid answer was yes — because it required exactly that. Ibn al-Zubayr upheld the righteousness of those who fought at the Camel without pretending the battle was not catastrophic, acknowledged the complexity of a period in which right people made costly errors without using that complexity as a reason to abandon the principles they had died for.
The Islamic tradition has answered these questions with fourteen centuries of evasion. Sunni orthodoxy largely sanctified whatever caliphate was in power, developed the doctrine of obedience to rulers as a necessity of public order, and pushed accountability into the eschatological future where it could not inconvenience anyone currently in office. Shia theology resolved the problem of legitimate authority by making the Imam's designation divine and thus unquestionable — which is not a solution to the problem of accountability but a sophisticated mechanism for avoiding it. Both traditions produced enormous civilizational achievements. Both, on this particular question, failed.
Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr did not fail. He was destroyed.
The distinction matters because a proof of concept that is destroyed by superior force has not been refuted. It has been suppressed. The argument Ibn al-Zubayr made — with his governance, his refusals, his death — has never been answered. It has only been silenced. Silenced by Hajjaj's catapults, by Umayyad historiography, by Alid frameworks that needed the Battle of the Camel to mean something other than what it meant, by a Sunni tradition that found quietism more sustainable than accountability.
The Last Subaltern does not accept the silence.
Ibn al-Zubayr built something real. He governed with a conscience that power, in fourteen centuries of Islamic political history, has rarely been required to maintain. He died defending the Ka'aba against the army of a caliph who had bombarded it. He refused every clean exit — theological insulation, martyrdom before governance, dynastic self-perpetuation, Kharijite purism — and chose instead the hardest path: the path of accountable authority, exercised imperfectly, under siege, for as long as it could be sustained.
It was sustained for nine years. Against an empire.
That is not a failure. That is an achievement. And it is an achievement that the tradition we have inherited — Umayyad, Abbasid, Alid, quietist Sunni — has collectively and systematically worked to ensure you never think of it that way.

Conclusion: The Account Is Still Outstanding
The caliphate of Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr ended on a hillside in Mecca in 692 CE when its founder chose death over submission to a dynasty that had bombarded the holiest site in Islam in order to reach him. It had been, for nine years, the functioning government of most of the Muslim world. It had been grounded in earned legitimacy rather than divine claim or dynastic inheritance. It had held the Camel as a standard rather than a stain — insisting that the demand for accountability is not discredited by the cost of making it. It had refused the Kharijite purity that would have required it to condemn its own founding principle. It had governed, imperfectly and under permanent existential pressure, by the discipline of moral answerability.
It was destroyed by Hajjaj ibn Yusuf's catapults and Abd al-Malik's army.
It was then erased by two historiographical traditions — Umayyad and Alid — that each found it inconvenient for different reasons and buried it accordingly.
What remained were: the Umayyad dynasty, which lasted until 750 CE; the Alid counter-tradition, which preserved Husayn's martyrdom as the paradigm of legitimate resistance; and the silence where Ibn al-Zubayr's governance should have been.
The silence is a political act, not a historical verdict. It was produced by power and sustained by institutions that benefited from it. It does not represent a judgment that the Zubayrid experiment failed the test of legitimacy. It represents the refusal of two traditions — each with reasons — to allow that test to be fully administered.
We administer it here.

Abdullah ibn al-Zubayr passes.

The traditions that erased him — and the powers that destroyed him — do not.
The account is still outstanding. It has been outstanding for fourteen centuries. We intend to keep presenting it until it is met.
